summary:
Galen of Pergamon (129–c. 216 AD) was the most important physician in ancient history after Hippocrates, and his medical writings dominated European and Islamic medicine for 1,300 years — a period of intellectual authority arguably unmatched by any other scientist. Physician to gladiators and later to the Roman emperor Marcus Aurelius, Galen produced an enormous body of work (over 2 million words survive) covering anatomy, physiology, pharmacology, pathology, and medical philosophy. His anatomical knowledge came primarily from dissecting animals (human dissection was not permitted in Rome), which led to important errors that were only corrected when Vesalius performed systematic human dissection in the 16th century.

Galen's physiological theory — combining Hippocratic humoral theory with Platonic philosophy and his own anatomical observations — posited that blood was produced in the liver, transformed by vital spirits in the heart, and consumed by the tissues. This was fundamentally wrong (William Harvey would demonstrate blood circulation in 1628), but it was internally consistent and provided a framework for understanding disease and treatment that proved remarkably durable. His pharmacological work systematically described hundreds of herbal medicines and their preparations — the term 'galenical' for plant-based medicines preserves his name in modern pharmacy. Galen's insistence on systematic anatomical observation as the basis of medicine, rather than pure philosophical speculation, represented a genuine scientific methodology whose influence outlasted the specific errors in his system.
